coredata.dc:title: NiCoPt/graphene-dot nanosponge as a highly stable electrocatalyst for efficient hydrogen evolution reaction in acidic electrolyte
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Ngoc-Anh
Ali, Yousuf
Nguyen, Van-Toan
Omelianovych, Oleksii
Larina, Liudmila L.
Choi, Ho-Suk
coredata.dc:description: This work reports the large-scale and simple synthesis of highly effective, stable, and cheap 3D nanosponge catalysts, nanoporous NiCoPt alloys wrapped in graphene dots (NiCoPt/G-dot), for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The simultaneous co-reduction of the metal precursors and carbon dots was confirmed by TEM, XPS, and Raman analyses. The SEM result indicated that the G-dots act as a structure-directing agent, assisting in the generation of the 3D nanosponge complex structure, in which the metal alloy constituents with the outer G-dots synergistically stimulate the electrochemical catalytic activity. Optimization of the alloy composition, while lowering the Pt loading, resulted in the highest HER catalytic activity by the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanohybrid with a precursor molar ratio of Ni:Co:Pt = 48:48:4. XPS analysis revealed that the nanohybrid surface was enriched with Pt (up to 86.7 at%), suggesting that the alloy composition is controlled by the tendency of Pt metal to segregate towards the surface. The resulting surface electronic structure led to the superior catalytic activity of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, which generated an overpotential of only 45.54 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in acidic solution. The Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot exhibited the highest catalytic activity, which correlated well with its having the lowest charge transfer resistance and the highest electrochemical surface area value of 57.51 cm2. The Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot showed good stability, retaining 94% of its current density after 21 h of operation. This was attributed to the existence of the G-dots, which acted as a protective layer. The developed 3D NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge may be considered a promising alternative catalyst for hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanosponge
Graphene dot
Transition metal alloy
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Stability
Electro-catalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrocatalytic performance analysis: screening of NiCoPt/Graphene-dot nanosponge catalysts for HER
The chemical state of the Ni48Co48Pt4/Graphene-dot nanosponge

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of the electrocatalytic activity of NiCoPt/G-dot samples synthesized with different molar ratios of Ni to Co, and commercial Pt/C catalysts. (a) Cyclic voltammetry curves measured in the range of −0.22 to 1.0 V vs RHE. (b) LSV polarization curves measured at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. (c) Tafel slopes and fitting curves of different catalysts. (d) The linear fitting curve shows the slopes of the different catalysts are equal to the double-layer capacitance (Cdl). (e) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopic curves measured at −0.2 V vs RHE and the corresponding circuit model. (f) Chronoamperometric curves of the NiCoPt/G-dot, NiCoPt nanoalloy, and commercial Pt/C simultaneously measured at −45.54, −52.67, and −41.60 mV, illustrating the durability of the synthesized catalyst in acidic solution.

SEM and TEM images illustrating the morphological and structural features of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot. (a) SEM image of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot showing the nanosponge-like structure. (b) TEM image of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge. (c) High magnification TEM image showing the thin graphene layer at the edge of the nanosponge. (d) High-resolution EDX mapping of the 3D nanosponge-like Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, confirming the presence of Ni, Co, Pt, and C elements in its structure. (e) HRTEM image of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge showing the layer of graphene dots. (f) The enlarged image (e) reveals a lattice spacing of 2.23 Å which corresponds to the (111) crystal plane. The inset of image (f) showing the SAED pattern of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot with the corresponding planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311).

High-resolution (a) Ni2p, (b) Co2p, (c) Pt4f, and (d) C1s spectra taken from Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dots nanosponge after mild etching with 0.5 keV ion beam energy for 100 s. Results of the deconvolution analysis. The solid dark blue lines indicate the fitted envelopes. The binding energy scale is referenced to the Fermi energy level. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Raman spectra collected for a range of D and G carbon bands for (a) C-dots and (b) Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dots. The bands were fitted with Gaussian curves.
Electrochemical performance data for NiCoPt/G-dot catalysts synthesized with different molar ratios of Ni to Co, and commercial Pt/C catalyst.
Atomic percentages of elements obtained from survey spectra taken from the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ∗ion beam energy of 0.5 keV, ∗∗ion beam energy of 3 keV.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Atomic percentages of metal elements obtained from survey spectra taken from Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ∗ion beam energy of 0.5 keV, ∗∗ion beam energy of 3 keV.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of fitting for Ni2p, Co2p, and Pt4f high-resolution spectra taken from the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.
Fitting results of the C1s high-resolution spectrum taken from the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ∗ion beam energy of 0.5 keV, ∗∗ion beam energy of 3 keV.

head.ce:title: NiCoPt/graphene-dot nanosponge as a highly stable electrocatalyst for efficient hydrogen evolution reaction in acidic electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Solar Photovoltaics, Institute of Biochemical Physics, Russian Academy of Sciences, Kosygin St. 4, 119334, Moscow, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Solar Photovoltaics, Institute of Biochemical Physics, Russian Academy of Sciences, Kosygin St. 4, 119334, Moscow, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports the large-scale and simple synthesis of highly effective, stable, and cheap 3D nanosponge catalysts, nanoporous NiCoPt alloys wrapped in graphene dots (NiCoPt/G-dot), for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). The simultaneous co-reduction of the metal precursors and carbon dots was confirmed by TEM, XPS, and Raman analyses. The SEM result indicated that the G-dots act as a structure-directing agent, assisting in the generation of the 3D nanosponge complex structure, in which the metal alloy constituents with the outer G-dots synergistically stimulate the electrochemical catalytic activity. Optimization of the alloy composition, while lowering the Pt loading, resulted in the highest HER catalytic activity by the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanohybrid with a precursor molar ratio of Ni:Co:Pt = 48:48:4. XPS analysis revealed that the nanohybrid surface was enriched with Pt (up to 86.7 at%), suggesting that the alloy composition is controlled by the tendency of Pt metal to segregate towards the surface. The resulting surface electronic structure led to the superior catalytic activity of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, which generated an overpotential of only 45.54 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in acidic solution. The Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot exhibited the highest catalytic activity, which correlated well with its having the lowest charge transfer resistance and the highest electrochemical surface area value of 57.51 cm2. The Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot showed good stability, retaining 94% of its current density after 21 h of operation. This was attributed to the existence of the G-dots, which acted as a protective layer. The developed 3D NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge may be considered a promising alternative catalyst for hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrocatalytic water splitting produces renewable hydrogen energy directly and accordingly is of high interest to scientists looking for new alternative methods of hydrogen production. Electrochemical water splitting is generally achieved using two different processes: photo-electrochemical electrolysis, and electricity-driven electrolysis. Electricity-driven water electrolysis involves two half-reactions, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [1,2]. Because HER has a sluggish nature and higher reaction activation energy, catalysts are required to stimulate the reaction rate and enhance the production of hydrogen [3].

At present, Pt is the most used highly active and stable electrocatalyst for HER, however, the high cost of this noble metal hinders its use on industrial-scales [4,5]. The development of Pt-free or low Pt-loading and stable electrocatalysts, which otherwise maintain the current level of catalytic activity for HER, is needed to overcome this challenge. Numerous studies have been undertaken to develop highly active electrocatalysts based on transition metals including Ni, Co, Cu, Fe, Mo, and Zn. Different types of transition metal-based alloys [6], sulfides [7], phosphides [8–10], carbides [11], and nitrides [12], which can work in harsh conditions such as acidic [13–15] and basic [16,17] media to produce hydrogen energy, have been suggested. However, these investigations seldom succeed, because of the high overpotentials generated by these catalysts.

Another approach to advanced catalysts is to boost the electrochemically active surface area of the electrocatalyst by designing different nanostructures with large surface areas, such as nanosponges, nanorods, nanoballs, nanocones, nanoneedles, and nanoflowers [18]. Compared to commercial Pt/C (activated carbon-supported Pt nanoparticles), these high surface area electrocatalysts can reduce the amount of Pt required. Several methods have already been employed for this purpose, including Pt monolayer-covered transition metal-based alloys, prepared using atomic vapor deposition [19–21]. The layered catalyst has shown electrocatalytic HER performance comparable to that of commercial Pt/C, due to the synergistic effects of the different transition metals alloyed with the Pt metal. Notably, the Pt–Pd-Gr stack structure, which was synthesized by controlling outer layer thickness, showed higher HER catalytic activity with decreased Pt layer thickness [22].

One approach to effectively achieve this performance involves the fabrication of Pt-loaded nanocomposite materials. Such methods can potentially reduce catalyst cost by eliminating a large amount of Pt. Our own early study confirmed that alloying Mo with Pt metal wrapped with graphene dots enhanced catalytic activity as well as the stability of the synthesized catalyst in both acidic and alkaline media [23]. The nanosponge-like structure provided a large amount of electrochemically active surface area and created numerous electron transfer routes, which allow easy electron transfer accessibility. Additionally, graphene dots improve the stability of the alloy, permitting long-term operation, by protecting it from decay [23]. Although considerable research has been conducted on the subject, developing low Pt-loading and stable electrocatalysts with state-of-the-art catalytic activity for HER remains a hot research topic for current water splitting technology.

In this study, a set of NiCoPt/G-dot nanoporous nanohybrids were synthesized and employed as inexpensive and stable electrocatalysts for HER. The synthesis of these nanohybrids with complex nanosponge structures was accomplished using a simple and low-cost one-step process. Optimizing the composition of the alloy while maintaining low Pt loading (0.04 mM) yielded the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanocomposite (with precursor molar ratio of Ni:Co:Pt = 48:48:4). The stable nanohybrid generates a small overpotential of 45.54 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in acidic solution. XPS surface analysis revealed the surface was enriched with up to 86.7% Pt. The chemical composition of alloy surface was Ni0.06Co0.07Pt0.87/G-dot. This finding suggests that the surface composition of the alloy is controlled by the tendency of Pt metal to segregate towards the surface.

Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99.99%), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 99.9%), chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O, 99.9%), and ethanol (C2H5OH, 99.99%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals were ACS grade reagents. Commercial Pt/C (activated carbon-supported Pt nanoparticles, 20 wt% Pt) and Nafion polymer solution (5 wt%, LIQUion 1100) were purchased from Alfa Aesar and Ion-Power, respectively. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was bought from ALS Co., Ltd., Japan. The area of the GCE was ∼0.071 cm2 with a diameter of 3 mm. Nitrogen gas was provided by Yonhap LPG (Korea).

NiCoPt/Graphene-dots (referred as NiCoPt/G-dot) were synthesized from the mixed precursor solutions and carbon dots (C-dots). The C-dots were synthesized using a procedure developed in our previous work [23,24]. Nickel (II) chloride, cobalt (II) chloride, and chloroplatinic acid were used as the Ni, Co, and Pt precursors, respectively. In this work, our primary goal was to develop a low-cost catalyst, thus the amount of Pt precursor used for the synthesis of the ternary alloy compound was fixed at 4 vol% (or 0.04 mM). To find the most suitable NiCoPt alloy composition of catalyst for efficient HER, catalysts with various ratios of Ni to Co were screened.

The set of mixtures were prepared consisting of Ni and Co precursor solutions with final concentrations of 0:0.96 mM, 0.24:0.72 mM, 0.48:0.48 mM, 0.72:0.24 mM, and 0.96:0 mM. The nanosponge composites were synthesized from the corresponding precursor mixtures. The resulting materials used as the catalysts are hereafter referred to as Ni96Co0Pt4/G-dot, Ni72Co24Pt4/G-dot, Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, Ni24Co72Pt4/G-dot, and Ni0Co96Pt4/G-dot.

A typical synthesis for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanohybrid can be described as follows. Nickel (II) chloride and cobalt (II) chloride were dissolved in 20 mL of DI water to get final concentrations of 0.48 mM for each reagent. Then, 2.5 mg of carbon dots were dispersed in this solution. Next, chloroplatinic acid was added to the precursor mixture to reach a concentration of 0.04 mM, and the obtained mixture was sonicated for 30 min to make a homogeneous yellow solution. After sonication, 1 mL of formic acid was added to the solution and the solution was sonicated again for 30 min. The obtained solution was kept at 70 °C for 10 h (Fig. S1). A reduction of precursor ions occurred along with the reduction of the carbon dots into graphene dots. Inserting the precursor ions into the layers of graphene dots enhances the stability of the nanoparticles.

The π-π interaction in graphene induces the formation of a nanonetwork. After 10 h, the precipitated black nanoporous material was taken out and washed several times sequentially with water and ethanol. After drying at 70 °C for 5 h, a blackish powder was obtained as the final product, a Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

For comparison, a sample of the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy without C-dots was synthesized by the same synthesis process. A schematic representation of the NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge synthesis process is shown in Fig. S2, where the formic acid acts as a weak reducing agent of the precursor ions and carbon dots. The carbon dots act as a structure-directing agent to obtain an electrocatalyst with a nanosponge-like morphology.

An X-ray diffractometer (Smart Lab, λ = 1.5406 Å, Cu Kα radiation, Rigaku corporation) was used to identify the crystallographic planes and crystallinity of the NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge. The synthesized NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800 with UHR lens) and by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100 F, 200 kV, JEOL LTD., Japan). The surface chemical state was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Thermo Fisher Theta Probe system equipped with a monochromated Al–K X-ray source with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV. (K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Avantage software was used for the analysis of the survey spectra and the C1s, Co2p, Ni2p, O1s, Pt4f high-resolution spectra. The core-level binding energy positions were determined as the center of the full width at half maximum of the photoemission peak using the Avantage software package. The fittings were performed by combining Shirley’s and Tougaard’s backgrounds.

To prepare the samples for the XRD, SEM, and XPS analyses, 4 mg of synthesized NiCoPt/G-dots was first dispersed in 1.0 mL of ethanol and sonicated for 20 min to make a homogeneous solution. The solution ink was then dripped onto silicon substrates and dried under an infrared lamp. A similar procedure was used to prepare the TEM sample, where the solution ink was dripped on a copper grid substrate.

A glassy carbon (GC) electrode covered with the synthesized NiCoPt/G-dot catalyst was used as a working electrode (WE) to measure electrochemical performance. A 25 mL stock solution of Nafion was prepared for this purpose by mixing 0.01 mL Nafion, 5 mL of isopropanol, and 20 mL of water. 2 mg of NiCoPt/G-dots was dissolved in 0.5 mL of the Nafion solution and sonicated for 30 min to prepare the catalyst ink. The Nafion polymer in the solution was used to attach the catalyst to the electrode surface. Prior to being covered with the NiCoPt/G-dot catalyst, the GC electrode was rubbed against polishing paper, and cloth. Then, a diamond (1 μm) and alumina (0.05 μm) suspension were used to make the electrode surface smooth and shiny. The polished GC electrode was washed and dried at normal temperature. Afterward, 3 μL of catalyst solution was dripped on the GC electrode and dried at a temperature of 70 °C. After proper drying, the electrode was cooled down to normal temperature.

To measure the electrochemical performance of the NiCoPt/G-dot catalyst, a three-electrode scheme with a rotating disc electrode was employed, using a potentiostat (IviumStat electrochemical analyzer from Ivium Technologies, Netherlands). A GC electrode covered with the synthesized NiCoPt/G-dot catalyst was used as the WE. A Ag/AgCl (3 M NaCl) electrode and a platinum coil were used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The electrochemical catalytic activity of the NiCoPt/G-dot was examined in acidic medium (0.5 M H2SO4) by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a fixed scan rate of 10 mV/s. The electrolyte was purged with ultra-pure nitrogen gas for 30 min before the measuremfvgdgdagents. A disruption potential amplitude of 10 mV was set to measure electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a frequency range of 0.1–105 Hz. The EIS was measured at a voltage of −0.20 V vs a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). An impedance analysis software was used to fit the EIS data for further analysis. In all electrochemical data, the voltage is referred to the scale of RHE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance analysis: screening of NiCoPt/Graphene-dot nanosponge catalysts for HER
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the chemical composition of the NiCoPt/G-dot on HER performance was assessed through a series of electrochemical measurements (Fig. 1 ). The catalytic activities of the NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge catalysts were evaluated using CV measurements, where the developed catalyst was employed as the WE. The CV was conducted in an N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Commercial Pt/C was employed as a benchmark material for reference. Fig. 1a shows the CV curves of the synthesized catalysts recorded in a potential range from −0.25 to 1.0 V. A typical set of redox peaks was detected. As can be seen, the highest oxidation and reduction peaks values were recorded for Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot at 0.89 and −0.19 V, respectively. The result confirmed that Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge exhibited the highest electrochemical active surface area, which contained the highest charge density for proton adsorption and desorption on the surface of the catalyst [23,25].

Fig. 1b depicts the electrocatalytic performance curves of the catalysts under study. The electrochemical activity of commercially available Pt/C was also evaluated to compare with the catalytic performance of the synthesized catalysts. The LSV polarization curve of the commercial Pt/C showed a small overpotential of 41.60 mV. In contrast, an overpotential of 45.54 mV was required for the as-synthesized Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge to reach the current of 10 mA/cm2. The overpotentials of Ni72Co24Pt4/G-dot, Ni24Co72Pt4/G-dot, Ni96Co0Pt4/G-dot, and Ni0Co96Pt4/G-dot were measured to be 57.98, 68.22, 76.27 and 112.12 mV, respectively.

To further characterize the synthesized catalysts, Tafel polarization curves were fitted and analyzed as shown in Fig. 1c. The corresponding Tafel slope value was quantified using the equation η = a + b log |j|, where j is current density, and b is the Tafel slope. The slope value is one of the fundamental characteristics of a catalyst and is an essential parameter for identifying the rate-determining step of HER. The lowest slope value was achieved for commercial Pt/C. Among the synthesized NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge samples, the minimum Tafel slope was obtained for Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, and was only 33.90 mV/dec (Table 1 ), which is very close to the value calculated for Pt/C (30.23 mV/dec). The obtained results proved the superior electrochemical activity of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

There are three possible steps involved in hydrogen gas generation in an acidic medium. The first step corresponds to the discharge of electrons (Volmer reaction), the second is desorption (Heyrovsky reaction) and the final step is the recombination step (Tafel reaction).
(1) H+ + e− → Hads (Volmer step)
(2) Hads + H+ + e− → H2 (Heyrovsky step)
(3) Hads + Hads → H2 (Tafel step)

Generally, a Tafel slope of 30 mV/dec means that the reaction proceeds through the Volmer-Tafel reaction mechanism. In the same way, a Tafel slope value of 40 mV/dec implies a Volmer-Heyrovsky reaction mechanism [26,27]. Since the slope values obtained for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge were lower than 40 mV/dec, we can conclude that the electrochemical recombination step is the rate-determining step, and the reaction follows the Volmer-Tafel mechanism (Eqs. (1) and (3)).

The electrochemical catalytic activity of a catalyst greatly depends on the active surface area, which provides favorable electron transfer routes, and stimulate the reaction kinetics [28]. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the synthesized catalysts were determined from the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) at the interface between the solid and liquid, as shown in Fig. 1d [29]. The calculation process is described in the Supplementary Information. The obtained Cdl and ECSA values are listed in Table 1. The highest surface area of 57.51 cm2 was determined for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge. In contrast, the other synthesized catalysts, Ni72Co24Pt4/G-dot, Ni24Co72Pt4/G-dot, Ni96Co0Pt4/G-dot, and Ni0Co96Pt4/G-dot, produced 42.77, 26.09, 18.33, and 12.54 cm2, respectively. These results provide evidence that the superior catalytic activity of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge is the result of its having the highest ECSA value, because of its porous surface and complex structure.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was conducted for the synthesized catalysts at frequency ranges from 0.1 to 105 Hz at a voltage of −0.20 V vs RHE. The corresponding Nyquist plots for Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, Ni72Co24Pt4/G-dot, Ni24Co72Pt4/G-dot, Ni96Co0Pt4/G-dot, and Ni0Co96Pt4/G-dot were measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte, as illustrated in Fig. 1e. The coinciding electrical circuit model is displayed in the inset. All of the synthesized catalysts generate one semicircle. The obtained charge transfer resistances (Rct) for the catalysts are listed in Table 1. The smaller value of Rct represents a better interface for charge transfer, suggesting more effective charge transfer through the catalyst/electrolyte interface, which enhances the electrochemical reaction [30]. The smallest Rct value of 29.05 Ω was obtained for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge. The Rct values for Ni72Co24Pt4/G-dot, Ni24Co72Pt4/G-dot, Ni96Co0Pt4/G-dot, and Ni0Co96Pt4/G-dot were calculated to be 61.06, 83.47, 97.60, and 116.90 Ω, respectively.

It is worth noting that the proton adsorption affinities of monometallic Ni and Co are similar. However, due to its higher electron affinity, electronegativity, and higher proton adsorption ability, the Ni-rich catalyst provides higher HER activity compared to a Co-rich catalyst [31]. Nevertheless, in the current work, we observed that when nanocomposites were synthesized using an identical molar concentration of Ni and Co precursors together in the presence of C-dots and a small amount of Pt precursor (precursor molar ratio of Ni:Co:Pt = 48:48:4), they showed superior catalytic activity and stability. The apparent reason for that may be the contribution of the Pt atoms to overall catalytic activity. The contribution of Pt atoms may be evaluated from surface chemistry data obtained by means of XPS. Indeed, the data obtained by XPS (Table 2 ) suggests intensive surface segregation of Pt atoms. The synergistic effects of Pt atoms and thin layers of graphene dot accelerate HER performance [23,32,33].

Chemical stability in the acidic electrolyte is an important requirement for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanohybrid catalysts for the HER reaction application. The electrochemical durability of the synthesized catalyst was evaluated in an N2-saturated 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. The stability of commercial Pt/C was also tested under the same conditions for reference purposes. In general, the transition metal-based catalysts are unstable and dissolved in the acidic solution after lengthy operation in the HER reaction. Consequently, a protective layer was employed to reduce the dissolution of the nanostructure during the HER reaction.

Nano-structure wrapping with a graphene sheet is an excellent technique that significantly improves the durability of nanohybrid materials [34]. Fig. 1f displays the chronoamperometric curves recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge, Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy, and Pt/C. The inset in Fig. 1f shows H2 generation and release during the chronoamperometric measurement. The curve of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge indicates it had a long-term stable nature, with more than 21 h of operation in the acidic electrolyte. No noticeable change in current density was observed during the test time, proving the high durability of the NiCoPt/G-dot nanosponge in an acidic environment. It retained 94% of the current density after 21 h, while showing excellent catalytic activity. Note that the catalyst’s curve behavior is similar to that of the Pt/C. In contrast, in the case of the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy, a 40% loss in current density was recorded after 18 h of operation. The obtained results indicate the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge can be considered a low-cost, stable, and efficient electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution.

To better understand the reasons behind the electrocatalytic analysis results, the sample of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot that possessed the best HER performance was carefully studied. SEM, TEM and HRTEM measurements were conducted for this purpose and the results are shown in Fig. 2 .

The SEM images revealed the morphology of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot (Fig. 2a), which was very porous in contrast to the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy (Fig. S3). TEM and HRTEM images of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge are given in Fig. 2b–f. The HRTEM images (Fig. 2c–e) reveal the thin layer of graphene at the edge of the nanosponge structure. This finding suggests the metallic nanoalloy catalyst has been wrapped with a protective graphene-dots layer. The graphene dots were generated via exfoliation of C-dot layers because tiny Pt clusters evolved while reducing Pt precursors were intercalated and grew inside C-dot layers, subsequently, C-dot layers were flaked off to cover Ni48Co48Pt4 nanosponge [23,35]. The lattice fringes with inter-planner distances of 2.23 Å corresponding to the (111) crystal plane, indicating a face-centered cubic (fcc) structure (JCPDS 04–0802) in the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge (Fig. S4) [35].

The aforementioned features are shown in the enlarged image in Fig. 2f. The inset image showing the selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot confirms the presence of (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes in the synthesized nanosponge, which is consistent with the XRD result as shown in Fig. S4 [36]. Clearly, as can be seen in Fig. S4, in the pattern of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge, there is a weak and broad peak at approximately 24.35° representing the crystal plane of (002). However, no peak was found at 24.35° in the pattern of the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy. The obtained result indicates the formation of graphene layers in Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, which wraps the synthesized catalyst and creates a sponge-like polycrystalline structure [35]. The HRTEM results are in good agreement with the XRD analysis results.

The energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) mappings (Fig. 2d) confirmed the existence of Ni, Co, Pt, and C elements in the sample, which were homogeneously distributed throughout the sponge-like nano-network. The EDS spectrum of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot provided the elemental composition. The EDS pattern of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot is given in Fig. S5. The comparatively higher intensity of the Pt peak indicates the Pt rich surface of the nanosponge. The result agrees well with the XPS data and supports the high HER-performance of this electrocatalyst.

The graphene wrapped 3D Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge with a highly porous and interconnected complex network exhibited a high surface area, containing an abundance of active sites. These multiple electrochemically active sites create inter-penetrating channels, which accelerate electron transfer, while the graphene layer protects the metal particles, thereby increasing stability during electrochemical hydrogen evolution [37]. Besides this, the highly comprehensive porous structure stimulates the diffusion of reactant molecules from the electrolyte within the surface of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge, resulting in the significant boost in reaction rate [38,39].

The beneficial effect of the graphene-dot wrapped nanosponge on HER activity was proven by direct comparison with the HER performance of Ni48Co48Pt4 with and without G-dots, shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7. In detail, Fig. S6 displays the CV curves recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy and Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, illustrating the superiority of the graphene-wrapped nanosponge. The CV study reveals that the graphene layers improve conductivity by generating electron transfer routes; consequently, an increase in peak height is observed for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot electrode. Furthermore, the LSV polarization curve analysis revealed that the overpotential value of 52.70 mV for Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy was higher than that for Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot (45.54 mV) (Fig. S7a). The obtained data prove that the G-dots covering of the nanosponge not only enhances durability, but also increases its electrical conductivity and provides a favorable catalyst/electrolyte interface for electron transfer from the electrode to the protons in the electrolyte.

The beneficial impact of the G-dot was further confirmed by the EIS, Tafel plots, and the double layer capacitance analysis. As can be seen in Fig. S7b, the Tafel slope of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot was 33.90 (mV/dec), which is smaller than the slope of 37.62 mV/dec recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy. The ECSA of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot is 57.51 cm2, which is larger than that of 45.71 cm2 of Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy (Fig. S7c). In addition, a smaller value of 29.05 Ω for Rct was deduced for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, compared to the Rct of 47.15 Ω recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy, suggesting a more effective charge transfer across the catalyst/electrolyte interface, which promotes the electrochemical reaction.

The comparative chronoamperomatric curves recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4 alloy and Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot are given in Fig. 1f. A 40% loss of current density after 18 h of operation was recorded for Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy, whereas the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge retained 94% of the current density after 21 h, showing excellent catalytic activity. The result illustrates the positive impact of the G-dots on the stability of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot catalyst in acidic solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The chemical state of the Ni48Co48Pt4/Graphene-dot nanosponge
ce:section.ce:para: The surface chemical states of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dots nanosponge were analyzed using XPS. Surface analysis requires a clean surface, which was realized using the Ar-cleaning procedure. It was proven that the remarkable alteration in the surface chemical state is strictly linked to the energy of the Ar ions. The widely used acceleration energy of 3 keV led to a partial reduction of the alloy nanoparticles, along with the removal of contamination [40]. Given that the Ni2+ oxidation state is readily reduced by Ar ion sputtering [41], to prevent any surface modification of the synthesized nanohybrid and to acquire reliable XPS data, we set a low Ar ion beam energy of 0.5 keV, and a short etch time of 100 s to clean the sample surface. To obtain the Pt, Ni, and Co concentration profile versus depth, we also collected XPS data after etching the sample with a high acceleration energy of 3 keV for 60 s.

A comparison of the XPS survey spectra taken from the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge in its as-received state, after mild etching (0.5 keV), and after etching the sample with the high acceleration energy of 3 keV are given in Fig. S8. The Ni2p, Co2p, Pt4f, O1s, and C1s core level emissions were recorded for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot sample. As can be seen, the intensity of the Pt 4f peak is considerably higher compared with those of Ni 2p and Co 2p peaks, indicating the surface of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot is Pt rich.

The elemental concentrations were determined from the survey scans at each etch level, and the atomic percentages of the elements are given in Table 2. The presence of contamination (a large amount of carbon) and Ar cleansing were characterized by the C1s core-level spectrum of the sample. A carbon concentration of 44.71 at% was recorded for the pristine surface, while only 25.43 at% was obtained after mild surface cleaning (level 2). This important observation confirms the existence of the graphene protective layer, which wrap the metallic nanoparticles. The gentle Ar sputtering is expected to remove ∼1–1.5 nm of the upper surface layers, which is very close to the thickness of the graphene protective layer, as suggested by the TEM results shown in Fig. 2e. The additional Ar etching with higher ion beam energy did not result in any noticeable changes in the C at%, suggesting that the surface of the nanoparticle is enriched with carbon. A similar tendency was observed for the O1s core level. The results confirmed that the spectra taken at level 2 provided reliable XPS information on the chemical state of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

The atomic percentages of metal elements obtained from the survey spectra of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge are given in Table 3 . As can be seen, the content of elemental Pt significantly exceeds the contents of Co and Ni at etching level 2 as well as at level 3. Indeed, a Pt atomic percentage of 86.69% was recorded, for while only 7.14 and 6.17% Co and Ni, respectively, were found in the survey spectrum of the nanohybrid after mild sputtering. Quantifying the spectra showed that the surfaces were Ni0.06Co0.07Pt0.87/G-dot at etching level 2, and Ni0.08Co0.09Pt0.83/G-dot at level 3.

The observed outcome (Table 3) leads to the conclusion that the molar ratio of the precursors in the original reagent mixture used for the synthesis of the nanosponge did not match the atomic composition of elements determined on the alloy surface. Despite the low loading of the Pt precursor (0.04 mM), the content of elemental Pt on the surface exceeded the Co (Co precursor, 0.48 mM) and Ni (Ni precursor, 0.48 mM) by a factor of 12 compared. The top layer (level 2) was enriched with up to 86.69% Pt, suggesting that the surface composition of the alloy was controlled by the tendency of Pt metal to segregate towards the surface (Table 3). The decrease in Pt content from 86.69 to 83.13 at% (level 3) after etching with an ion beam energy of 3 keV supports the conclusion of surface Pt segregation. Note that the Co and Ni contents were increased (7.14–8.67 at% of Co and 6.17 to 8.20 at% of Ni).

The obtained results are in line with experimental and theoretical studies on the subject [41]. The set of experimental data clarified the surface segregation and segregation-induced changes in the surface electronic properties, which did not appear in the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dots nanosponge.

To identify the chemical state of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge, the core-level emissions of related compounds were monitored. Peak fitting and deconvolution were done for Ni2p, Co2p, Pt4f, and C1s high-resolution spectra using Thermo Avantage software (Thermo Scientific). The results of the fitting for samples after etching with energies of 0.5 keV and 3 keV, as well as for a pristine sample, are presented in Fig. 3 , and Figs. S9, S10, S11, S12. The fitting was done by combining Shirley’s and Tougaard’s backgrounds. The deconvolution parameters are listed in Table 4 . Peak areas have been converted to atomic percentages.

The Ni2p spectrum for the sample taken after mild etching (0.5 keV) and its deconvolution are given in Fig. 3a. The spin-orbital splitting of Ni2p3/2 - 2p1/2 accounted for 17.5 eV Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 are located at energies of 855.0 and 872.5 eV, respectively. Ni2p3/2 and Ni2p1/2 satellites are visible in the spectrum. The Ni2p spectrum was decomposed into two components. The peaks at binding energies of 855.0 and 851.1 eV are assigned to the Ni2+ oxidation state and Ni0 metal state, respectively [41]. The atomic percentage of Ni0 exceeds with that of the Ni2+ oxidation states by a factor of 2 (Table 4).

Fig. 3b shows the Co2p spectrum and its deconvolution. The Co oxidation state was distinguished using satellite features at 784.0 and 786.4 eV of the Co2+ spectrum. The spin-orbital splitting Co2p3/2 - 2p1/2 of around 15.1 eV was recorded accordingly with two satellites [41]. The deconvolution result indicated that 63.5 at% of the Co remained in the Co2+ oxidation state, and 37.5 at% in the metal state (Table 4).

As can be seen in Fig. 3c, the Pt4f spectrum has well separated spin-orbit components with spin-orbital splitting Pt4f7/2–4f5/2 of 3.3 eV which is in line with the literature [41]. The Pt4f peak formed by two overlapping peaks indicates that Pt is present in Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge in the two chemical states. The peak at a binding energy of 71.9 eV is related to the Pt2+ oxidation state while the peak at 70.9 eV can be assigned to the Pt0 metal state. The assignment aligns with data reported elsewhere [41].

The existence of the Pt0 and Pt2+ oxidation states is a result of the reduction of H2PtCl6 during the synthesis process. It is seen that the emission intensity of the Pt2+ is considerably less than that of the Pt0 metal state (Fig. 3c). The atomic percentage of Pt0 exceeds the Pt2+ oxidation states in the first outermost layer of nanohybrid (level 2) by a factor of 2.4 (Table 4).

To identify the change in the chemical state of the Pt in the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot, and in pure Pt, the Pt4f core level emission was recorded for monometallic Pt nanoparticles (Fig. S13). A shift in the Pt4f7/2 peak around 0.2 eV toward the Fermi energy level for the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot was recorded. It was proven that, for the transition metal series, the shift in the surface core-level of the host metal was accompanied by a similar shift in its valence d-band center. Therefore, the center of the d-band of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot was shifted toward the Fermi energy, resulting in the depopulation of the antibonding states [42]. Thus, an increase in the catalytic activity of the nanosponge electrode can be expected, due to the electronic effect that arises from an upshift in the Pt d-band upon alloying [43,44].

Thus, the Ni0, Co0, and Pt0 metal states in the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge result from the reduction of precursors (NiCl2, CoCl2, and H2PtCl6) during the synthesis process. The obtained atomic percentages of the Ni0, Co0, and Pt0 metal states suggest the high efficiency of the co-reduction process. The higher percentage of Pt0 metal state could be attributed to the high rate of Pt4+ and Pt2+/Pt reduction, compared to that of Ni2+ and Co2+ due to the significantly higher standard electrode potentials of the Pt4+/Pt and Pt2+/Pt.

The obtained outcome in conjunction with the tendency of the Pt to segregate towards the surface of the nanosponge leads to Pt enrichment of the surface by more than 80%, and in turn, the modified surface electronic properties of the nanohybrid affect its catalytic activity. Because of the distribution of oxygen-containing groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the graphene, the Pt2+, Ni2+, and Co2+ oxidation may occur on the edge of the aromatic domains, leading to an increase in electrical conductivity.

The C1s core level emission and its deconvolution are given in Fig. 3d and its fitting results are listed in Table 5 . The C1s spectrum of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge was decomposed into four components: C=C (aromatic rings), C–C (aliphatic), and oxygen-containing functional groups, -COOH (carboxyl) and C–OH (hydroxyl). The peak positions are in agreement with data reported elsewhere [45].

The oxygen-containing functional groups, such as the carbonyl (C=O) and epoxy (C–O–C) components were not recorded in the spectra of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge, while some of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were reflected in minor components (Fig. 3d, Table 5). We found that synthesis yields the high atomic percentage of 60.2% for C=C bonding, which highlights the large aromatic domain size, suggesting structural damage on the basal plane of the graphene is low [46]. Thus, our XPS results suggest the developed composite materials possess high conductivity, which positively affects the catalytic activity of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge.

The partial reduction of pristine carbon dots to graphene dots, induced by the co-reduction of the Ni/Co/Pt precursors and carbon dots during the synthesis of the Ni48Co48Pt/G-dots nanosponge, was confirmed by Raman spectroscopy analyses. The Raman spectra of the C-dots and the Ni48Co48Pt/G-dots nanohybrid collected in the range of the D and G carbon bands are shown in Fig. 4 . The difference in the structure of the two samples reflects the Raman spectra. The spectra were fitted with Gaussian curves. Both spectra revealed the first-order allowed tangential G band centered at 1587 cm−1 and the disorder-induced D mode at 1357 cm−1. The G mode is related to the in-plane motion of C sp2 atoms in both aromatic and olefinic molecules. The D peak intensity is related to the presence of six-fold aromatic rings. It is well known that the area ratio of the D and G bands varies with the size of the sp2 cluster size [47]. We found that the ID/IG area ratio was 0.84 for C-dots and 0.65 for Ni48Co48Pt/G-dots. The decrease in the ID/IG ratio indicates a rise in the average size of the sp2 clusters in the Ni48Co48Pt/C-dots. Thus, during synthesis, the dimensions of the sp2 clusters in a network of sp3 and sp2 bonded carbon are increased. This result is linked to the reduced structural damage on the basal plane of the graphene, due to the loss of oxygen upon reduction, and the exfoliation of the graphene dots around the nanoparticle to form nanosponge structure [33,48]. Indeed, the oxygen-containing functional groups form sp3 bonds with the carbon atoms in the basal plane. In this way, the degree of clustering of the sp2 is increased in conjunction with sp2 bond restoration during the synthesis of the Ni48Co48Pt/G-dots nanosponge. The obtained results agree well with the XPS data discussed above.

In summary, we developed a facile and cost-effective synthesis method for the fabrication of a highly stable nanoporous structure, composed of NiCoPt alloy wrapped with graphene dots. The surface electronic structure could be tuned via adjustments in the molar ratio of the Ni/Co precursors. Low Pt loading (4 vol% in precursor mixture) in the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanocomposite was maintained, while producing the highest catalytic activity for HER yields. In spite of the low Pt loading, the surface of the developed nanocatalyst was enriched with Pt atoms, as evidenced by XPS. Quantifying the survey spectra showed that the surface atomic composition was Ni0.06Co0.07Pt0.87/G-dot, suggesting that the surface composition of the alloy is controlled by Pt metal segregating towards the surface.

The experimental results provided evidence that one of the reasons for the superior catalytic activity of Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge was the highest ECSA value of 57.51 cm2. The data also confirmed that the graphene wrapped 3D nanosponge exhibited a higher surface area than the Ni48Co48Pt4 nanoalloy. The CV study revealed that the graphene layers improved conductivity and created favorable electron transfer routes.

The practical value of the Ni48Co48Pt4/G-dot nanosponge does not rely solely on its high efficiency, but stability as well. The as-synthesized nanocatalyst was tested for more than 21 h in an acidic environment, and it maintained an impressive 94% of its initial catalytic activity. Thus, the graphene wrapping of the Ni48Co48Pt4 not only enhances durability but also improves electrical conductivity and provides a favorable interface for electron transfer from the electrode to the protons in the electrolyte. Numerous active sites and efficient charge transfer routes throughout the active surface area have the effect of lowering the charge transfer resistance, leading to efficient electron transfer.

Implementing the derived optimal chemical composition led to the synthesis of robust 3D nanosponge catalysts with catalytic activity for HER comparable to the activity of commercial Pt/C. Both the developed nanohybrids and the synthesis process are cost-effective. The outcome of our study allows us to state that the developed 3D nanosponge is a promising alternative catalyst for hydrogen production.

Ngoc-Anh Nguyen: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yousuf Ali: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Van-Toan Nguyen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Oleksii Omelianovych: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Liudmila L. Larina: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Ho-Suk Choi: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a National Research Foundation (NRF) grant (NRF-2020R1A4A2002590; NRF-2020R1I1A1A01072495) and a Brain Pool Program (NRF-2019H1D3A2A01062040) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT, Republic of Korea.
